planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6 to 8 weeks before last frost; surface-sow onto moist seed-starting mix and keep at 60 to 75 degrees Fahrenheit until germination, then transplant out after frost danger has passed.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last expected frost, pressing them onto the surface of a moist, well-draining seed-starting mix at 60 to 75 degrees Fahrenheit; do not cover deeply as light aids germination. Transplant seedlings into a sunny site with sharply drained, lean soil after all frost risk has passed, spacing plants 18 to 24 inches apart to allow airflow around the aromatic foliage. Once established, Persian Germander is highly drought-tolerant and thrives on neglect — avoid rich soil and heavy watering, which promote floppy growth at the expense of the dense, upright flower spikes. Cut plants back by about one-third after the first flush of bloom to encourage a second wave of the deep purplish-red flowers that are so attractive to bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring just as new growth emerges; replant divisions at the same depth in well-drained soil and water in well to establish.

directions: Because Faassen's Catmint is a sterile hybrid, propagate it by dividing established clumps in early spring just as new growth begins, or take stem cuttings in late spring; replant divisions promptly in well-drained soil in full sun. Site in average to lean, dry soil — fertile or overly moist conditions produce floppy, open growth rather than the neat mounding habit this plant is prized for. Shear the entire plant back by half immediately after the first heavy flush of bloom in late spring to promote a dense second flush of lavender-blue flower spikes through summer and into fall. Divide clumps every three to four years to keep them vigorous, as the center of older plants can die out over time.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall so they undergo natural cold stratification over winter; press seeds onto the soil surface and cover lightly, as they benefit from a period of cold before germinating in spring.

directions: Sow seeds directly in fall onto the surface of prepared soil in a lightly shaded, calcareous or neutral spot, covering them with a thin layer of fine grit or soil; the cold of winter breaks dormancy and promotes reliable germination the following spring. Plants establish slowly in their first season, focusing energy on root development, so expect the tall, woolly-leafed stems and whorled purple flower spikes in the second year. Choose a site with good drainage and moderate moisture — Alpine Woundwort tolerates partial shade under open tree canopy where it would naturally occur in mountain scrub and woodland edges. Leave spent flower stems standing through winter to provide structure and overwintering habitat for beneficial insects, then cut back to the base in early spring before new growth emerges.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds 2 to 4 weeks before the last expected frost date into well-drained, alkaline or neutral soil; barely cover seeds and thin seedlings to 18 to 24 inches once established.

directions: Sow seeds directly into their final position in early spring, 2 to 4 weeks before the last expected frost, in a sunny site with well-drained, lean, preferably alkaline or neutral soil; cover seeds very lightly and thin seedlings to 18 to 24 inches apart once they are large enough to handle. Downy Woundwort demands excellent drainage and suffers in heavy, wet soils — raised beds, gravel gardens, and south-facing slopes suit it well and show off the intensely felted, silver-white foliage to its best advantage. Plants flower in their second year, sending up stems of pale pink, whorled flowers that attract bees; leave a portion of the flower heads to ripen for self-sowing. Cut back dead stems in late winter and apply a grit mulch rather than organic material to keep the base dry and prevent crown rot during wet winters.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow cold stratification over winter; press seeds onto the surface of short-mown or lightly raked soil and leave uncovered, as seeds need light and moisture contact to germinate well in spring.

directions: Broadcast seeds onto short, closely mown grass or lightly raked bare soil in fall, pressing them into good contact without covering; winter cold stratifies the seed naturally so that germination occurs reliably the following spring. Cut-leaved Selfheal thrives in dry, calcareous grassland conditions — once established it is drought-tolerant and competes well among low meadow plants, making it an excellent candidate for wildflower turf or lawn alternatives. Allow flower heads to mature and set seed before mowing so the colony can spread and self-sow; mow no lower than three inches to protect the low rosettes. Where it co-occurs with common selfheal (Prunella vulgaris), expect natural hybridization producing intermediate flower colors ranging from white to pale lilac.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds 2 to 4 weeks after last frost into moist, humus-rich soil in a shaded or partially shaded location; seeds require warm soil temperatures of 65 to 80 degrees Fahrenheit to germinate well.

directions: Sow seeds directly into moist, humus-rich soil in a shaded or woodland-edge location 2 to 4 weeks after the last frost, when soil has warmed to at least 65 degrees Fahrenheit; barely cover seeds with fine soil and keep the area consistently moist until germination. Hairy Ruellia is best suited to partially shaded spots with reliably moist, fertile soil — stream banks, rain garden edges, and damp woodland borders closely mimic its native habitat in the southeastern US forests. Plants establish slowly in their first season but develop into upright, hairy-stemmed clumps that produce lavender-blue trumpet flowers from summer through early fall, drawing hummingbirds and sphinx moths. Deadhead spent flowers to extend bloom and reduce aggressive self-seeding; in frost-prone areas at the northern edge of its range, apply a light mulch over the crown in late fall to protect the root system through winter.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
